coredata.dc:title: Multichannel detection of persulfate by fluorescent carbon quantum dots derived from one-pot solvothermal reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Dong
Xu, Hu
Long, Qin
Yu, Jun
Wang, Yuhong
Zhang, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Here we report the multichannel detection of S2O8 2− by fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in aqueous media. The one-pot reaction derived yellow emitting CQDs (Y-CQDs) and red emitting ones (R-CQDs) were used to establish the multichannel detection platform (i.e. fluorescence on–off, colorimetric, and ratio fluorescent/colorimetric) for S2O8 2− based on their different responses to S2O8 2−.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The detection of S2O8 2− via fluorescence on–off/colorimetric mode
The detection of S2O8 2− via ratiometric fluorescent/colorimetric mode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS full-scan spectrum of Y-CQDs (a), the high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (b), O 1s (c), N 1s (d), and S 2p (e), and TEM image and HRTEM (the insert) of Y-CQDs (f).

Fluorescence spectra of Y-CQDs with different concentrations of S2O8 2− (a, the insert presents the linear plot of F550/F0 versus S2O8 2− concentrations (the insert, 20–300 μM) in (a) process. UV–Vis spectra of Y-CQDs with different concentrations of S2O8 2− (b, the insert stands for the linear relationship between A534/A0 and S2O8 2− concentrations. Fluoresce spectra of Y-CQDs/R-CQDs with different S2O8 2− concentrations (c, λex = 450 nm, pH 5). The insert represent the corresponding fluorescent photographs of Y-CQDs/R-CQDs suspension under UV lamp and the linear relationship between F570/F624 and S2O8 2− concentrations. UV–Vis spectra of Y-CQDs/R-CQDs (2:1) with different concentrations of S2O8 2− (d, the insert represent the photographs of CQDs with different concentrations of S2O8 2− under daylight and the linear plot of A534/A610 vs. different S2O8 2− concentrations).
Schematic illustrations of synthesis of fluorescent CQDs and S2O8 2− detection by CQDs.
Detection performance of CQDs for S2O8 2− in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Multichannel detection of persulfate by fluorescent carbon quantum dots derived from one-pot solvothermal reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Applied Catalysis, School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, Shanghai 201418, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Institute of Applied Catalysis, School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, Shanghai 201418, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Birdo (Shanghai) Pharmatech Co., Ltd, Shanghai 201318, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Birdo (Shanghai) Pharmatech Co., Ltd, Shanghai 201318, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multichannel detection platforms for S2O8 2− by fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been developed. The detection of S2O8 2− was realized by one-pot derived CQDs via fluorescence on–off, colorimetric, ratiometric fluorescent, and ratiometric colorimetric modes. The pure water phase application environment and ratiometric colorimetric/fluorescent detection modes facilitate our detection platforms to have broader application prospects.

Here we report the multichannel detection of S2O8 2− by fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in aqueous media. The one-pot reaction derived yellow emitting CQDs (Y-CQDs) and red emitting ones (R-CQDs) were used to establish the multichannel detection platform (i.e. fluorescence on–off, colorimetric, and ratio fluorescent/colorimetric) for S2O8 2− based on their different responses to S2O8 2−.

ce:section.ce:para: Persulfate anion (S2O8 2−) has been widely used in the advanced oxidation process (AOP) of organic pollutants, textile bleaching, and etching of printed circuit board [1,2]. So real-time monitoring S2O8 2− concentration is highly required [3]. Till now, traditional iodometric titration [4], reduction method [5], polarographic spectrophotometry [6], chemiluminescence method [7], and ion chromatography [8] etc. have been developed to detect quantitatively S2O8 2−. However, the accuracy and the time consuming of sample preparation and test by sophisticated equipment are always the obstacle. Optical detection methods may be promising for S2O8 2− detection [9–15]. For instance, Gokulakrishnan et al. used N,N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DPD) as colorimetric agent to realize the visual S2O8 2− detection via the oxidative of DPD by S2O8 2− [9]. But these works were performed by direct observing the change in solution color or fluorescence intensity. Moreover, the signals were unidirectional changes, either enhanced or weakened without no large lateral shift or the reverse change of dual optical signals, which would bring inevitably a certain false positive probability. Furthermore, the tedious preparation and purification of organic chromophoresor fluorophores always limited their practical applications [13,14].

Recently, CQDs have been widely utilized in various fields including drug transport, bioimaging, fluorescent probes, photocatalysis due to easy preparation, low-toxicity, and good biocompatibility etc. [16,17]. However, much more CQDs associated with S2O8 2− focused on the AOP of pollutants [18]. To today, only one report focused on CQDs-based probe for S2O8 2−, in which a dual emissive CQDs-Zn2+ nanocomposite was firstly constructed and the subsequent S2O8 2− addition induced an opposite fluorescence spectral change [19]. However, the relatively low sensitivity as well as non-pure water system will greatly hinder its practical application. Herein, we reported multichannel detection of S2O8 2− by Y-CQDs and R-CQDs via one-pot reaction (see supplymentary material). Based on the difference in optical response of Y-CQDs and R-CQDs to S2O8 2−, four channel detection platform, i.e. fluorescence on–off, colorimetric, and ratio fluorescent/colorimetric, were readily established (Scheme 1 ). To our knowledge, this is the first case of S2O8 2− detection in multichannel mode by fluorescent CQDs. The economy of one-pot reaction, pure water application environment, and ratiometric detection modes enable the platforms to have broader application prospects.

Due to the same reaction conditions, it is expected that the similar structure and surface groups of Y-CQDs and R-CQDs are almost same, so the characterization of CQDs was mainly represented by Y-CQDs. Fig. 1 a showed the full XPS spectrum of Y-CQDs, which demonstrated four elements i.e. C (284.7 eV), N (400.5 eV), O (531.2 eV), and S (168.2 eV) existing in our Y-CQDs. The high-resolved spectrum of C 1s showed three peaks centered at 284.6, 285.9, and 287.2 eV standing for C–C, C–N, and CO bonds (Fig. 1b), respectively. The O 1s signals at 530.8 and 532.1 eV corresponded to O–H and C–O–C, respectively (Fig. 1c). The peak of N 1s could be deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 400.5 and 398.8 eV corresponding to N–H and C–H, respectively (Fig. 1d). The four signals of S 2p at 169.6, 168.4, 163.3, and 161.8 eV could be assigned to SO4 2−, C-S, and S2− moieties (Fig. 1e). The XPS results and FTIR information (Fig. S1) indicated N,S co-doped Y-CQDs were successfully synthesized. The as-prepared Y-CQDs exhibited a dot-like morphology (average diameter 5.4 nm) with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1f), which implied the mono-dispersion of Y-CQDs after purification. The optical properties of CQDs, stability experiments, the effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of CQDs as well as the structural information of R-CQDs were also investigated (Figs. S2–S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of S2O8 2− via fluorescence on–off/colorimetric mode
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, the optical property of Y-CQDs was very sensitive to S2O8 2−. Thus, Y-CQDs could be developed to a fluorescent (and colorimetric) probe for S2O8 2− via fluorescence on–off mode (solution color change). Under the optimal detection conditions (pH 4.0), the fluorescence of Y-CQDs was gradually decreased with the successive addition of S2O8 2− (Fig. 2 a). Simultaneously, a new absorption band appeared at 534 nm and its intensity was increased successively during S2O8 2− addition (Fig. 2b). We inferred that the strong oxidative ability of S2O8 2− was responsible for these spectra changes (Figs. S7–S9) [20]. For fluorescent probe, a linear plot could be obtained by plotting F/F0 factor vs. S2O8 2− concentration as shown in the insert in Fig. 2a (the absorbance ratio of A534/A0 vs. S2O8 2− concentration for colorimetric assay, A534 and A0 represent the absorbance of Y-CQDs at 534 nm after and before adding S2O8 2−, respectively. See the insert in Fig. 2b). Based on IUPAC 3σ criterion, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 8.3 μM (fluorescence mode) and 1.2 μM (colorimetric assay) for S2O8 2−, respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for three repeated tests with 220.0 μM of S2O8 2− were 0.15% (fluorescence mode) and 0.48% (colorimetric assay), which demonstrated the considerable reproducibility. In addition, such a fast color change (ca. 24 s) reflected the fast reaction kinetic characteristic between Y-CQDs and S2O8 2− (Fig. S10). Thus, this was really favorable for real time and on site S2O8 2− detection. Furthermore, the detection performance by Y-CQDs was comparable to the most of other optical S2O8 2− sensors (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of S2O8 2− via ratiometric fluorescent/colorimetric mode
ce:section.ce:para: Unexpectedly, R-CQDs, as a side product of Y-CQDs, was relatively inert to S2O8 2−, which could be ascribed to the slightly higher HOMO of R-CQDs (-5.794 eV) than the reduction potential of S2O8 2− (-5.89 eV in this case). The native recombination of photogenerated electron/hole pairs of R-CQDs occurred more easily upon excitation (Fig. S8). Thus, it was expected that a ratiometric fluorescent/colorimetric probe could be constructed based on the different responses of Y-CQDs and R-CQDs to S2O8 2−. By mixing Y-CQDs and R-CQDs (molar ratio 2:1), the ratiometric fluorescent/colorimetric platform Y-CQDs/R-CQDs for S2O8 2− could be easily established. To ensure the certain detection sensitivity in ratiometric detection, the pH value of CQDs suspension was adjusted to 5. Meanwhile, the excitation wavelength was set at 450 nm. As a result, the fluorescence color of Y-CQDs/R-CQDs suspension exhibited a series of fluorescence color transition from green to orange and finally to red upon adding S2O8 2− (the insert in Fig. 2c). For ratiometric colorimetric assay, a new absorption signal appeared at 534 nm (stemming from Boyland-Sims oxidation [20]) and its intensity was increased gradually with the continuous addition of S2O8 2− (0–400 μM), which led to the color of CQDs suspension converting from pale violet to purplish red (Fig. 2d). A more evident color change was shown in the insert of Fig. 2d after dilution. The linear relationship between the intensity ratio of two emission peaks (F570/F624) and S2O8 2− concentration (20–400 μM) as shown in the insert in Fig. 2c (the absorbance ratio A534/A610 vs. S2O8 2− concentration for ratiometric colorimetric assay, see the insert in Fig. 2d). The LOD was calculated to be 2.1 μM for ratiometric fluorescent (2.3 μM for ratiometric colorimetric) assay by IUPAC 3σ criterion. A 6.6% of RSD (three repeated tests with 220.0 μM of S2O8 2−) for ratiometric fluorescent mode (0.42% of RSD for ratiometric colorimetric mode) demonstrated the good application reliability. Importantly, our ratio type assays adopted the mutual validation of two signal sources, which could significantly eliminate the impact of uncontrollable external factors to realize the detection accuracy. At this point our detection assay is considerable advantageous over the others (Table S1). In addition, the multichannel detection platform exhibited the relatively high selectivity to S2O8 2− (Fig. S11). The actual detection performance for S2O8 2− by our multichannel detection platform in tap water was quite excellent (Table 1 ). The presence of possible components including Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, and Al3+ etc. as well as sulphate did not interfere with S2O8 2− detection, which also demonstrated the practicability of our multichannel detection platform for S2O8 2−.

In summary, we have developed the multichannel detection platform for S2O8 2− by using fluorescent Y-CQDs and R-CQDs as optical reporters. This work not only provides the great economical effectiveness by one-pot reaction derived CQDs, but also provides the facile multichannel detection platform suitable for in time and on site S2O8 2− detection.

Dong Xu: Investigation, Data curation, Software, Writing – original draft. Hu Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Qin Long: Investigation, Data curation. Jun Yu: Writing – review & editing. Yuhong Wang: Conceptualization. Peng Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21808142) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2022.132183.
